skill.name: cellxgene-census
skill.description: Query CZ CELLxGENE Census data for single-cell genomics analysis. Build filters, retrieve metadata and expression data, and use scanpy or PyTorch workflows.
skill.summary: Query and analyze CZ CELLxGENE Census single-cell data with reproducible Python workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found they are Markdown fences, inline backticks, and domain terms in documentation examples. No prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration, destructive filesystem behavior, or executable skill scripts were found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Benign External Command Guidance
low_findings.description: The only real shell commands reviewed are uv pip install examples for installing the public cellxgene-census package and optional dependencies. They do not include shell interpolation, remote script piping, credential handling, or destructive commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are visible setup instructions and use fixed package names. The command pattern exists, but the context is ordinary package installation rather than command injection or malware behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern False Positives in Documentation
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are caused by Markdown code fences, inline backticks, Python dictionary access, and biological metadata names. The reviewed examples demonstrate CELLxGENE Census queries and analysis workflows rather than executable marketplace code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is Markdown documentation and Python examples for data access. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes these snippets automatically or hides commands in them.

content.value_statement: Single-cell genomics analysis requires precise filters and careful memory management. This skill guides CELLxGENE Census queries, metadata exploration, and analysis workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use CELLxGENE Census for human and mouse single-cell data.
Provides filter patterns for cell type, tissue, disease, genes, and primary data selection.
Shows metadata-first workflows before loading expression matrices into memory.
Guides AnnData retrieval for focused queries that fit in memory.
Describes out-of-core processing and PyTorch dataloader patterns for larger analyses.
Documents scanpy integration and common troubleshooting steps.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include executable helper scripts or APIs.
Users must install and run the CELLxGENE Census Python package themselves.
Large Census queries can require significant memory, storage, and network bandwidth.
Scientific conclusions still require validation by qualified domain experts.

use_cases.title: Explore tissue cell types
use_cases.description: Find available cell types, diseases, donors, and tissues before deciding which expression data to load.

use_cases.title: Build reproducible expression queries
use_cases.description: Create versioned Census filters for marker genes, primary cells, and selected metadata columns.

use_cases.title: Prepare machine learning datasets
use_cases.description: Use Census query patterns with PyTorch dataloaders for cell type classification or representation learning.

prompt_templates.title: Find available cells
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cellxgene-census skill to show how I can list available cell types in human lung tissue without loading expression data.

prompt_templates.title: Create a focused AnnData query
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cellxgene-census skill to design an AnnData query for primary human T cells in COVID-19 samples with CD4, CD8A, and FOXP3.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a large query
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cellxgene-census skill to plan an out-of-core query for brain cells and explain how to process expression batches safely.

prompt_templates.title: Design an ML workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cellxgene-census skill to outline a PyTorch dataloader workflow for training a cell type classifier from Census data.

output_examples.input: Find cell types in human lung tissue.
output_examples.output: A metadata-first workflow that opens the Census, filters human lung primary cells, selects the cell_type column, and summarizes counts.

output_examples.input: Analyze marker genes for immune cells.
output_examples.output: A focused query plan using gene filters, primary data selection, relevant metadata columns, and guidance to validate gene availability.

output_examples.input: Avoid memory errors for a large brain query.
output_examples.output: A batch-processing approach using axis queries, selected genes, estimated cell counts, and incremental statistics instead of full matrix loading.

best_practices: Start with metadata queries before loading expression matrices.
Include is_primary_data filters unless duplicate cells are part of the study.
Specify a Census version for analyses that must be reproducible.
anti_patterns: Loading broad Census queries into AnnData without estimating result size.
Mixing Census versions within one analysis without documenting the difference.
Assuming every gene is measured across every dataset without checking presence.

faq.question: Does this skill run CELLxGENE Census queries for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You run the Python package in your own environment.

faq.question: Which organisms does it cover?
faq.answer: The documented Census workflows focus on Homo sapiens and Mus musculus data.

faq.question: Can it help with scanpy workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to load Census data into AnnData and continue with common scanpy analysis steps.

faq.question: Can it help with machine learning?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents PyTorch dataloader patterns for Census data, including batch size and label column choices.

faq.question: How should I avoid duplicate cells?
faq.answer: Use the is_primary_data field in filters unless your analysis intentionally studies duplicates.

faq.question: Why should I specify a Census version?
faq.answer: A fixed version helps make results reproducible when the public Census changes over time.
